card.title: Giving Learner Home's navigation the structure it never had.
card.subtitle: Simplified Learner Home's navigation to reduce friction and align the experience across platforms.

sections.title: How do you navigate between what users say and what users do?
sections.subtitle: Kaplan is a global educational services company. I simplified Learner Home's navigation to reduce friction and align the experience across platforms.

meta.team: Product Designer · UX Researcher · Product Manager · Lead Engineer

sections.label: THE PROBLEM
sections.title: Learner Home had too many ways to get anywhere. None of them obvious.
sections.body: Learner Home was cluttered with overlapping menus, duplicate functionality, and unclear hierarchies. Crucial actions were hidden behind a hamburger menu, and the learners' experience across platforms wasn't in harmony for access to their courses, certificates, and account settings. The navigation had grown organically — and it showed.

image.alt: Kaplan Learner Home before the redesign

sections.label: USER INTERVIEWS
sections.title: Users said one thing, did another.
sections.body: I designed two options: a consolidated layout that unified all actions into a clear hierarchy, and an incremental approach that maintained consistency with others. We partnered with users to spend most of their time. Before committing to re-engineering effort, I tested both with 7 users to see if the effort was worth it.

sections.title: Users navigate by quick links. Not the primary nav.
sections.body: Across both options, participants consistently used the content cards on the page rather than the menu to complete tasks — confirming that page layout carries as much navigational weight as the nav itself.

sections.title: Placement decides whether a CTA works.
sections.body: Same label, same task — 72% failure rate in Option 2 vs. 100% success in Option 1. Position, not naming, was the deciding factor.

sections.title: Preference and behavior pulled in opposite directions.
sections.body: 78% of users preferred Option 2. But Option 1 consistently outperformed it in task completion. We sided with behavior over stated preference.

sections.title: Can a treasure hunt fix a broken loyalty loop?
